However, I believe that long term investors can take advantage of this decline and begin accumulating shares.

If you've followed me for some time, you're probably aware that I typically focus on dividend paying stocks and ETFs. While I do value income generation within my portfolio, I am also very bullish on the growth of the Technology sector over the next decade, mostly due to the growth of the AI sector. Technology companies aren't typically known to prioritize dividends for shareholders, so may dividend funds lack a meaningful Technology allocation. The quickest and most efficient way for me to get the Technology exposure I seek is through an ETF like MGK.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund's most recent overview, MGK has net assets totaling $25B and is spread across 69 different holdings. MGK seeks to track the performance of the CRSP US Mega Cap Growth Index, which represents the companies that make up 70% of the cumulative capitalization of the US Total Stock Market. The fund is passively managed so rebalances and reconstitutions are done on a quarterly basis so that the fund is always up to date with its holdings.

Since MGK tracks the largest growth stocks, it comes as no surprise that the holdings are most heavily allocated towards the Technology sector, making up 52.27% of assets. This is followed by weight in Consumer Cyclical and Communication investments, accounting for 16.25% and 12.49% of assets respectively. However, an investment in MGK is essentially an investment in Technology. Since Technology makes up over half of the fund, the sector has the greatest influence on MGK's daily price movement and total return potential.

Since MGK only maintains exposure to about 69 holdings, the fund is a lot more focused than some peers. For instance, the top ten holdings reveals that Apple (AAPL) is the largest single position at 13.96% of assets. This is followed by an 11.18% weight in Microsoft (MSFT) and a 10.96% weight in NVIDIA (NVDA). The combination of these three holdings makes up over 36% of assets, while the top ten holdings makes up about 63% of assets.

I specifically want this more focused approach because it eliminates the unwanted fluff that a total market ETF may have, such as Vanguard's Total Stock Market Index Fund ETF (VTI) that has over 3,000 holdings within. I believe that the more focused portfolio can lead to outperformance over the long term.

As you are probably aware, many of the top holdings within MGK have seen double-digit price growth over the twelve months. Looking at the chart below, MGK has essentially captured a blended growth rate in comparison. While you may be better off simply choosing to initiate positions in the top performers, this requires a greater capital commitment and there's no guarantee that the top performers will remain the top performers over the next decade. Here are some notable recent events related to some of these top holdings:

  • Tesla (TSLA): Elon Musk will step down from his lead government role, which may help reverse the downward trajectory TSLA has experienced, YTD.
  • NVIDIA (NVDA): Currently in talks to acquire Lepton AI, a company that rents out servers. NVDA has an average Wall Street price target of $171 per share, indicating a potential upside of nearly 55%.
  • Meta Platforms (META): Announced that the company's Llama artificial intelligence models have been downloaded more than 1 billion times, proving its growing competitiveness.

Performance Comparison

As I said at the beginning of this article, it can be very difficult to determine which ETF to choose. While I don't typically care too much if a fund outperforms another by a few percentage points, MGK has outperformed some more notable funds with a wider margin. There are several peers that take a similar approach to focusing on growth stocks, but MGK outperforms the following funds over the last decade. While the underlying weights, expense ratios, and sector allocations may vary, these ETFs all pursue a similar goal. Here are the fund stats laid out next to each other for easy visibility:

Vanguard Mega Cap Growth Index Fund ETF (MGK)

  • Expense ratio: 0.07%.
  • 69 holdings
  • $25B in AUM (assets under management)

Schwab US Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG)

  • Expense ratio: 0.04%.
  • 229 holdings
  • $34.8B in AUM

Fidelity Enhanced Large Cap Growth ETF (FELG)

  • Expense ratio: 0.18%
  • 104 holdings
  • $3B in AUM

iShares S&P 500 Growth ETF (IVW)

  • Expense ratio: 0.18%
  • 216 holdings
  • $51.5B in AUM

SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 Growth ETF (SPYG)

  • Expense ratio: 0.04%
  • 212 holdings
  • $30.5B in AUM

The only notable difference to MGK's outperformance may be attributed to its much smaller amount of holdings. MGK has done a great job at assessing large-cap companies and choosing an efficient weight towards each of its top holdings. Assuming positive growth going forward, MGK is the fund that I want to continue accumulating throughout 2025 and beyond.

The trade off of a tighter focus of holdings is that MGK has demonstrated a greater downside potential during market declines. For instance, market indexes declined throughout 2022 as a result of a Fed rate shock. The Fed aggressively hiked interest rates in an attempt to combat the higher than anticipated inflationary data. MGK saw a greater loss than all of these peer ETFs. However, I am personally okay with this tradeoff since I plan to hold MGK for a longer time horizon.

Outlook & Risk

I personally utilize AI tools to some degree in plenty of aspects of my life already. I own a Google Pixel Phone (GOOG), which comes with AI editing software for pictures that I use on a weekly basis. I own a pair of Meta's Ray-Ban (META) smart glasses that have AI capabilities which allows me to send messages without even pulling out my phone. I play video games listed on Microsoft's (MSFT) Game Pass service, that uses AI to build procedurally generated worlds. These are just some of the real-world examples that are already relevant in today's world. While these implementations may seem small, there is a future where the utility of AI is so much more advanced.

Imagine self-driving robo-taxis from Tesla (TSLA) or Uber (UBER) that can get implemented on a global scale. We may see a world where AI-driven robotics assist doctors with complicated medical procures. AI can improve infrastructure related to urban planning and sustainability efforts, which will improve the quality of our lives. Then there's the whole AI aspect of blockchain technologies in the crypto world.

MGK makes it easy to align yourself to benefit from these innovations since many of the top holdings are investing in products or services that leverage AI technology. Additionally, there are several research papers that suggest there is still massive growth ahead for the sector. For instance, Markets & Markets estimates that the size of the AI sector can increase at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 35.7% through 2030.

However, there have been contradictory reports that believe the average annual growth of markets can slow in the future, driven by slower sales growth of these technology companies. Goldman Sachs released a report that estimated the S&P 500 will average an annual return of 3% for the next decade, which is a far way off from the +10% average the market has grown accustomed to.

The idea here is that it's simply unsustainable for these companies to continue reporting double-digit sales growth year after year. For instance, only 11% of S&P 500 companies have been able to maintain a double-digit sales growth for more than 10 consecutive years. While I think this outlook is overly-pessimistic, it's certainly a possibility.

Perhaps we see a market environment that begins to question the growth of AI markets. There's a risk that it will take longer than anticipated for growth to materialize throughout the technology sector, accompanied by growing competition overseas. For instance, we saw the negative market reaction as a result of DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) earlier in the year. There's a chance that investor sentiment around the sector changes, which can cause capital to flow out of tech and into other areas of the market.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate MGK as a buy as the fund is an efficient way to instant gain exposure to the highest quality large-cap companies in the world. If you have a bullish long-term perspective on the growth of AI and technology, MGK can be a way to capitalize on the future growth. The markets recently pull back caused by ongoing tension related to Trump's tariff proposals has presented an attractive opportunity for accumulation. MGK has a fewer amount of holdings compared to peer ETFs, but I believe this can ultimately lead to outperformance as it eliminates the extra fluff. The fund is also very affordable, with a low expense ratio of 0.07%.

data.title: SOXX ETF: An Attractive Entry Point To The Mainstream Adoption Of AI
data.description:
The performance of iShares Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ:SOXX) remains far from encouraging due to the bumpy ride of the main semi players since the beginning of the year. My previous piece on SOXX pointed out the 5G networks and GPU demand for data centers as the main pillars for the upside potential. I believe that SOXX at current levels is an attractive Buy opportunity ahead of the semiconductor market growth. My estimate stipulates up to 35% upside potential, which could come from the fund's reasonable exposure to top-tier semiconductor manufacturers in light of the recent market conditions, emphasis on analog chip providers, and an attractive valuation.

Fund overview

SOXX ETF is established to track the performance of the ICE Semiconductor Index, which consists of stocks of the leading semiconductor manufacturers and equipment providers. The fund remained quite concentrated around its top 5 holdings, which account for 37.5% of the fund's portfolio of 30 companies. However, there is a shift in the core positions with Texas Instruments (TXN) occupying 8.1% of the portfolio, followed by Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) with 7.7% share; Broadcom (AVGO) with 7.4%; Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) with 7.3%; and Qualcomm (QCOM) with 6.6% share, where Intel (INTC) was dropped from the top 5 holdings. In the meantime, the net asset value of the fund grew by 24.1% since my previous article up to $10.8 billion, which costs the investors 0.35% on annum.

Looking at the performance since the beginning of the year, the semiconductor stocks and the technology sector as a whole have been on a bumpy ride. SOXX has tumbled 13% to date and is now about 29% down from its record high. The funds direct competitors, VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) and First Trust Nasdaq Semiconductor ETF (FTXL) share the same direction, while SPDR S&P Semiconductor ETF (XSD) and Invesco Semiconductor ETF (PSE) fell further.

The reasoning behind the lagging performance of the semiconductor and the technology sector as a whole this year could be linked to trade disputes about tariffs and the worries about future investment levels in AI following the outbreak of DeepSeek’s models. However, the mainstream adoption of GenAI is yet to take place, where the hardware infrastructure spending is anticipated at least to double in the near term in order to meet the AI workloads. Hence, I believe that the current downfall of semi-socks represents an attractive buying opportunity going forward.

Industry dynamics

Considering the industry condition, global chip sales marked an 18% YoY increase in January, maintaining momentum following the strong semiconductor market in 2024. The recent data confirmed the continued steady annual growth rates of semiconductor sales, but WSTS expects the semiconductor market to slow down somewhat during the year.

Still, according to forecasts, chip sales should increase by 11.2% YoY in 2025 following 19% growth last year. If we try to make a linear interpolation, the forecast implies a 1.3% monthly sequential growth during the year (dotted blue line). We can also notice that the blue line is positioned higher than the green and red lines, indicating that the market could hit new highest-ever sales numbers for each month. The growth pattern provides quite encouraging prospects for the semiconductor players, where the memory and logic segments should rebound the most.

Industry risk factors

The semi-industry is affected by the technological standoff between the US and China, which continues to escalate. The tariff hike on imports from China is expected to put pressure on the PC and smartphone markets, creating a risk of a decreased demand for semiconductor chips. In addition, the release of DeepSeek’s AI models triggered significant volatility in the market and uncertainty about the future scale of investments. The spread of such cost-efficient models could lead to a slowdown of investments in large data centers and reduce the demand for advanced chips.

Investment inference

I will keep to the weighted average target prices estimation approach based on securities that make up the fund’s portfolio in order to assess the SOXX potential. Taking the consensus estimates, the model infers a significant upside potential for the SOXX ETF of around 35%, which implies a target price of $256.

We can notice that the majority of the holding has significant gains anticipated by analysts, which could bring SOXX near to its all-time high level. In particular, the largest contribution to the estimated upside could come from Nvidia (4.22%), Broadcom (3.31%), AMD (3.09%), Marvell (2.35%), and Qualcomm (1.96%). It’s obvious that the main upside should be delivered by semiconductor manufacturers, since they are the prime drivers of artificial intelligence development. In addition, the fund has more than 25% of its portfolio invested in analog chip providers, which are expected to face a strong increase in demand this year.

Overall, such a strong forecast for semi-players supports the opinion that the AI boom hasn’t come yet. The rapid development of the technology requires a lot of computing power to run the advanced models. What’s more, the emergence of more cost-efficient models could accelerate the diffusion of AI in various sectors of the economy, thus further stimulating the demand for computing power for inference operations.

Conclusion

In my opinion, SOXX provides an incredible opportunity to buy at a discount the expected AI hardware spending growth. Compared to SMH, the fund has far less exposure to NVDA and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), which could facilitate investors to avoid the current uncertainty regarding the AI investments and the escalation of the China-Taiwan tensions.

The valuation of SOXX exhibits a downward trend since the beginning of the year, where the forward P/E decreased by 10.4% to 20x. Going forward, the latter ratio is still 10% below the 3-year median of 22.2x and is lower than the broad technology select sector’s 23.4x valuation represented by XLK. As a result, I will keep my Buy rating on SOXX as the ETF has an attractive valuation, providing a good entry point to capture the anticipated growth of the semiconductor market in 2025.

data.title: CMNIX: An Opportunity For Ballast In Turbulent Markets
data.description:
By Eli Pars, CFA

During the first quarter, markets once again took investors on a roller coaster ride. The market’s anticipation of business- and market-friendly policies (e.g., lower taxes and less regulation) gave way to anxiety about the impact of tariffs on inflation, consumer activity, and economic growth. The S&P 500 fought back from a 10% peak-to-trough decline, but still finished down more than 4% on the quarter, with a significant rotation away from tech stocks. After a disappointing 4Q loss and a lackluster 2024, the bond market made its way back into investors' good graces, benefiting from the market’s flight to areas of the market with perceived safety.

Through it all, Calamos Market Neutral Income Fund (CMNIX) continued to deliver on our aim of providing steady gains, regardless of the market’s direction. Our approach — combining an arbitrage sleeve and hedged equity sleeve — has little exposure to interest rate opportunity or risk. During the quarter, this resulted in the fund’s advance trailing traditional bonds slightly, but over time, our approach has delivered a better — and more consistent — historical return experience than traditional fixed income.

The Case for CMNIX

Outperforming Bonds over the Long Term

The Appeal of the “Middle Road”: CMNIX Versus Stocks and Bonds

Growth of $10,000, six months to 3/31/25

How We Are Positioned

Our strategy gives us the flexibility to adjust our allocation on an ongoing basis. Especially in fast-moving markets, we believe this gives Calamos Market Neutral Income Fund an edge over funds with more static rebalancing strategies. Throughout the quarter, we took advantage of volatility and were active in booking profits and rebalancing our hedges. Our hedged equity allocation crept up at the margin. Both our convertible arbitrage and hedged equity strategy contributed positively to performance, with the former leading.

CMNIX Allocation

Hedged Equity. This sleeve is positioned to provide upside participation in the equity market, while seeking to significantly limit downside exposure. The volatility of the first quarter gave us opportunities to lock in an attractive spread between the calls we were writing and the put spreads we created; overall, our hedge remains toward the more defensive end of its long-term historical range, although slightly less defensive than at points in recent quarters.

Arbitrage. Our arbitrage sleeve is built around a core allocation to convertible arbitrage, with opportunistic allocations to other strategies, such as merger arbitrage and SPAC arbitrage. Although the fund’s allocation to convertible arbitrage decreased marginally during the period, our outlook remains constructive.

The first quarter saw brisk new issuance, with companies bringing nearly $23 billion in new issuance to market globally, with structures that were attractive on the whole. Looking forward, although issuance could be tempered if equity markets stay volatile, the interest-rate environment continues to incentivize issuers to choose convertibles over nonconvertible debt to contain borrowing costs.

Global Convertible Issuance: Healthy Year-to-Date Levels ($bil)

Our allocations to merger arbitrage and special purpose acquisition company (SPAC) arbitrage held steady over the quarter. Both are highly idiosyncratic strategies. We expect to see the Trump administration turn more attention to lowering regulatory burdens in the coming months, which could boost the opportunity set in merger arbitrage.

Outlook

The magnitude and reach of President Trump’s April 2 tariff announcement took markets by surprise, and we’re seeing a violent reaction in the immediate wake. However, since Trump 2.0 began, we’ve seen that nothing is set in stone when it comes to tariffs. And while it’s hard today to look past tariffs and the potential “worse-case” impacts on the markets and economy, other areas of Trump’s policy agenda could provide some countervailing tailwinds.

No doubt, there will be a lot of twists and turns, which we believe play to our strengths and the potential opportunity of Calamos Market Neutral Income Fund. One of our bedrock principles is that we can’t predict what will happen in the markets, so we need to position the fund for as many outcomes as possible. To us, this seems like an especially good approach in the current environment.
